coredata.dc:description: The concurrent fabrication of packaging materials with minimal additive concentrations, flame retardancy and superior thermal conductivity has consistently posed a significant challenge for materials scientists. Herein, a zero-dimensional carbon dot (CD) doped with nitrogen (N) and boron (B) elements was synthesized to serve as a thermal conductive filler. Subsequently, diethyl aluminum hypophosphate (ADP) and this doped CD were assembled via electrostatic interactions, resulting in the formation of ADP@CD. And a multifunctional epoxy resin thermoset material (EP/ADP@CD) with smoke suppression, toxicity reduction and excellent thermal conductivity was prepared using ADP@CD as fillers. Notably, when the combined addition of ADP@CD was restricted to merely1.5 wt%, with a ratio of ADP to CD being 1: 0.5 wt%, the EP/ADP@CD thermosets achieved a V-0 rating in the vertical flame (UL-94) test, despite a lower overall flame retardant concentration. Furthermore, it exhibited a limiting oxygen index (LOI) of 36.1 %. Meanwhile, compared to pure EP, EP/1.5ADP@CD thermosets exhibited a reduction in total heat release (THR), total smoke release (TSP) and carbon dioxide emissions (CO2P), which was as high as 25.8 %, 25.6 % and 40.1 %, respectively. The mechanical characteristics of the EP thermosets were little impacted by the extremely small additions of ADP@CD. Meanwhile, the EP/1.8ADP@CD (ADP:CD = 1:0.8 wt%) thermosets achieved a thermal conductivity of 1.1 Wm−1 K−1, which was 302 % higher than that of pure EP. In conclusion, this study provides a new option for constructing a new thinking for the exploitation of low addition amounts and multifunctional epoxy resins.

head.ce:title: Construction of flame retardant functionalized carbon dot with insulation, flame retardancy and thermal conductivity in epoxy resin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The concurrent fabrication of packaging materials with minimal additive concentrations, flame retardancy and superior thermal conductivity has consistently posed a significant challenge for materials scientists. Herein, a zero-dimensional carbon dot (CD) doped with nitrogen (N) and boron (B) elements was synthesized to serve as a thermal conductive filler. Subsequently, diethyl aluminum hypophosphate (ADP) and this doped CD were assembled via electrostatic interactions, resulting in the formation of ADP@CD. And a multifunctional epoxy resin thermoset material (EP/ADP@CD) with smoke suppression, toxicity reduction and excellent thermal conductivity was prepared using ADP@CD as fillers. Notably, when the combined addition of ADP@CD was restricted to merely1.5 wt%, with a ratio of ADP to CD being 1: 0.5 wt%, the EP/ADP@CD thermosets achieved a V-0 rating in the vertical flame (UL-94) test, despite a lower overall flame retardant concentration. Furthermore, it exhibited a limiting oxygen index (LOI) of 36.1 %. Meanwhile, compared to pure EP, EP/1.5ADP@CD thermosets exhibited a reduction in total heat release (THR), total smoke release (TSP) and carbon dioxide emissions (CO2P), which was as high as 25.8 %, 25.6 % and 40.1 %, respectively. The mechanical characteristics of the EP thermosets were little impacted by the extremely small additions of ADP@CD. Meanwhile, the EP/1.8ADP@CD (ADP:CD = 1:0.8 wt%) thermosets achieved a thermal conductivity of 1.1 Wm−1 K−1, which was 302 % higher than that of pure EP. In conclusion, this study provides a new option for constructing a new thinking for the exploitation of low addition amounts and multifunctional epoxy resins.

ce:section.ce:para: As a thermosetting polymer compound, epoxy resin (EP) plays an important role as a thermal management material in the fields of electronics and electrical appliances, as well as aerospace [1–3], due to its advantages such as low curing deformation rate and excellent electrical insulation properties. However, with the development of electronic devices and 5 G technology, the inherently low thermal conductivity [4,5] (k values of 0.2 W m−1 K−1) of EP severely limited its application as a thermal management material. Simultaneously, fire hazards are triggered due to a large amount of heat accumulation, so epoxy resin exhibits flammability and releases a large amount of smoke particles [6,7] during combustion limit its application [8–10]. The addition of thermal conducting fillers and other additives into the epoxy aim to the preparation of high-performing thermal conductivity epoxy coatings, though these latter may negative impact on electronic packaging materials on the conductivity property. It is still a great challenge to obtain multipurpose, thermally conductive and insulated EP thermosets which meanwhile reduce smoke and harmful gas emissions.

At present, the common flame retardant method of epoxy resin is used flame retardant for the introduction of flame retardants containing flame retardant elements or flame retardant groups, such as halogen flame retardants [11], phosphorus flame retardants [12–15] and nitrogen flame retardants [16,17]. The halogenated (HX) and other gases released by halogenated flame retardants [18] during the combustion process are poisonous and extremely corrosive, and their usage has been restricted in many countries and regions. Phosphorus-containing flame retardants are excellent alternatives to halogenated flame retardants. For example, Min et al. [19] added modified aluminum diethylphosphonate (K-ADP), 9,10-dihydro-9-oxa-10-phosphaphenanthrene-10-oxide (DOPO) and charring-forming agent (CFA) to epoxy resin at the same time. At a total addition rate of 14 %, the obtained thermosets material achieved UL-94 V-0 rating and the LOI was increased to 32.2 %. Chai.et.al [20] prepared metal–organic frameworks modified ammonium polyphosphate (APP@Cu-MOF) by coupling agent. The LOI of the EP composite containing 9 wt% APP@Cu-MOF was measured to be 29.4 %. Additionally, the TSP and CO2P were reduced by 54.7 % and 30.0 % respectively, in comparison to pure EP. Unfortunately, the high levels of loading of phosphorus flame retardants have a greater impact on the mechanical properties of polymer materials and a large amount of smoke will be released during the combustion process. Hence, it is imperative to decrease the overall quantity [21] of flame retardant additives and inhibit the production of smoke by harnessing the synergistic impact of various flame retardants.

On the other hand, in order to enhance the thermal conductivity of EP, the related research mainly classified into intrinsic thermal conductive polymers and filled thermal conductive polymers [22] according to the preparation process. The filler modification technology effectively improved properties such as insulation strength while improving thermal conductivity. Currently, the main thermally conductive and insulating fillers commonly used include alumina [23,24], boron nitride [25,26], graphene [27–29], zinc oxide [30,31] and carbon nanomaterials [32]. Among them, carbon nanotubes [33–36](CNTs) as carbon-based flame retardants had been used as synergists to improve the fire safety of phosphorus-containing flame retardant systems due to their wide range of sources, good charring properties and biological non-toxicity. As a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nano-filler, carbon dots (CD) can provide excellent flame retardancy [37–41] at very low loading due to its huge specific surface area and catalytic char formation. Aurelio et.al [42] reported a multifunctional flame-retardant epoxy thermosets obtained by dispersing 0.3 wt% Hexadecyltrimethoxysilane (HDTMS)-functionalized CD into diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA) resin, and the time to flaming combustion of EP composite was increased up to 76 %. Wang et.al [43] obtained a phosphorus-nitrogen synergistic flame retardant system (SPFRS) by blending NP-CQD prepared from citric acid and hexachlorocyclic melamine as precursors with ammonium polyphosphate (APP). When 8 % SPFRS (NP-CQD: APP=1:9 wt%) was added to EP, the flame-retardant EP (EP/CQD-APP2:18, CQD: APP=2:18 wt%) obtained passed UL-94 V-0 and achieved 33.2 % of LOI. Ronn Goei et.al [44] prepared nitrogen-modified carbon dots (NCDs) by means of citric acid and ethylenediamine. A transparent transverse thermally conductive film was prepared by adding NCD to a cellulose-based solution and drop casting onto FTO glass. Therefore, the application of CDs as flame retardants is receiving more and more attention. Although nanoparticles are expected to bring significant enhancement to EP performance, the challenge still remains that nanofillers often lack active groups and are difficult to interact with EP macromolecular chains, resulting in poor compatibility between nanofillers and EP, and difficulty in uniformly dispersing within EP, which greatly reduces and restricts the use performance of EP thermosets.

In order to solve the above problems, an epoxy resin thermoset with a low additive amount of flame retardant, thermal conductivity and low smoke toxicity was prepared in this thesis. N, B-CD was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using citric acid as C source, urea and borax as N and B sources. Further, the ADP@CD resulting from the combination of CD and ADP by electrostatic adsorption was introduced into the EP substrate, which was prepared to obtain EP/ADP@CD thermosets. At the same time, the chemical composition and microscopic morphology of CD, as well as the flame retardancy, thermal conductivity, electrical insulation and mechanical properties capabilities of EP/ADP@CD thermosets were detected using various characterization means. In addition, the flame retardant mechanism and the thermal conductivity mechanism of EP thermosets were explored in depth. This study further expands the application of CD and presents a novel approach for the preparation of multifunctional flame retardant EP thermosets.

Ethanol absolute was purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Critrazinic acid (CA) was bought from Tianjin Zhiyuan Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium tetraborate decahydrate (Borax) was purchased from Tianjin Ruijinte Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea was provided by Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 4,4’-Diaminodiphenylmethane(DDM) was bought from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Epoxy resin(EP, epoxy equivalent 184–195 g/mol, color≤40, viscosity 10000–16000 m·Pa·s) was purchased from Jiangsu Nantong Xingchen Synthetic Material Co., Ltd., Aluminum diethylphosphinate (ADP) was provided by Shandong shian Co., Ltd. All water used in the experiment was deionized water

N, B-CD was synthesized by hydrothermal method in one step, as shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, A total of 1.9 g of citric acid, 0.6gof urea, and 1.1 g of borax were weighed and combined with 40 mL of deionized water. After stirring and sonication until complete dissolution to a clarified transparent solution, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and then heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The supernatant was further filtered with 0.22 μm cellulose filter membrane to remove impurities. Further, the liquid portion was treated with aqueous dialysis for a duration of 24 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da in order to create a solution containing carbon dot. The carbon dot solution was freeze-dried and grounded to create CD powder.

Scheme 1 showed the preparation process of EP thermosets. Firstly, different proportions of CD and ADP were weighed and dispersed into 25 mL of acetone according to Table S1, and sonicated for 1 h until they were dispersed into a homogeneous mixed solution to obtain ADP@CD. Further, EP was introduced into the ADP@CD mixed solution and subjected to mechanical stirring for a duration of 1 hour. After removing the acetone, the heated melted curing agent DDM was added to the continuously stirred EP and stirring was continued for 15 min. Subsequently, the concoction was relocated to a vacuum oven set at a temperature of 80°C and subjected to degassing for a duration of 15 min in order to eliminate any air bubbles that may have formed throughout the stirring procedure. The degassed mixture was carefully transferred to an aluminum paper mold. EP were pre-cured at 100°C for 2 h and at 150°C for 2 h to obtain flame retardant EP thermosets in a blast oven. The samples obtained were designed as EP-xADP@yCD (x and y represent the mass fraction).

The morphology and lattice spacing of the CD powder were examined using a Transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM 2100). The functional groups on the surface of the CD powder were identified using Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) in the wavelength range of 4000–650 cm−1 with 64 scans and a resolution of 6 cm−1. The elemental composition of the CD surface was further examined by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with Al-Kα target rays as the X-ray source with a scanning range of 0–1200 eV under an ultra-high vacuum system at a base pressure of 1.0 × 10−8 mbar. The crystal structure of CD was determined using X-ray Diffraction (XRD) with a scanning rate of 5°/min. A scanning micro-morphology of the thermosets cross-section and the residual carbon after cone calorimetry tests were analyzed using a Field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM; JSM-7500F). Before SEM analysis, gold was spray-coated using a JEC-3000FC ion sputterer. The surface elemental distribution of microstructure and residual char were observed by Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) and the EDS analysis was carried out by Oxford X-Max electric refrigeration energy spectrometer (EDS Inca X-Max; England). The heat loss of the samples was measured using Thermo-Gravimetric Analysis (TGA) under a nitrogen(N2) atmosphere at 50–800°C with a heating rate of 20 °C/min. The mechanical properties of the samples were assessed by Dynamic thermomechanical analysis (DMA; DMA8000, Perkin Elmer) using a double cantilever mode. The temperature was increased from 25 to 300 °C with a nitrogen flow rate of 20 mL/min at a rate of 5 °C/min while maintaining a constant frequency = 1 Hz, and a vibration amplitude of 10.0 μm.

The vertical burn test (UL 94) was conducted by CZF-3 (Jiangning Analysis Instrument Co., Nanjing, China). The size of the sample was 130 mm×13 mm×3.2 mm. The limiting oxygen index (LOI) was determined by JF-3 (Jiangning Analysis Instrument Co., Nanjing, China). The size of sample was 130 mm×6.5 mm×3.2 mm. The combustion test of the sample was carried out by the Cone calorimeter test (CCT) under the thermal radiation power of 50KW/m2 (Jiangning Analysis Instrument Co., Nanjing, China). The size of sample was 100 mm×100 mm×3 mm. The smoke production of the samples was assessed using a Plastic Smoke Density Tester (SDT) under conditions of thermal radiation and flameless combustion at a power density of 25 kW/m2 (Yangyi Walch Inspection Technology Co., Suzhou, China). The mechanical properties of EP composites were evaluated by the RGT-20A universal testing machine (Shenzhen, China) at 25 °C and a speed of 5 mm/min. The substances resulting from the thermal cracking of the sample were identified using Pyrolysis-Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Py-GC-MS; EGA/PY-3030D). The Pyrolysis temperature was set to 500°C. The ramp-up technique involved initially maintaining the column temperature at 50°C for 1 min, followed by a gradual increase to 300°C at a rate of 10°C per minute, and then holding it at 300°C for 1 min. The degree of graphitization of the residual char was tested by Laser Raman spectrometer (Raman; SPEX-1430) using a 514 nm laser with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The thermal conductivity of the material was measured using a Thermal Conductivity Analyzer (TCi; H357, Canada) on samples with 100 mm diameter and samples of 5 mm diameter using the standard ASTM D7984. The gas-phase flame retardant mechanism of the samples was analyzed using Thermogravimetric Analysis with Infrared Spectroscopy (TG-IR; TGA8000, PerkinElmer). The analysis was conducted by gradually increasing the temperature at a rate of 20 °C per minute, ranging from 50 to 800 °C. A nitrogen flow rate of 40 mL per minute was maintained during the analysis. Surface temperature changes in thermosets during heating and cooling were documented using the infrared thermal imager (Testo 781). The volume resistivity of the samples was measured with a resistance measuring instrument (ZC-90G, Taiou Electronic Co., Shanghai, China) at 100 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, surface, and optical properties of CD
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and dimension of the obtained CD were characterized by TEM, as shown from Fig. 1 a and b. The prepared CD nanoparticles exhibit monodisperse spherical like properties with a particle size of around (2.75±0.34) nm. The lattice spacing of the CD was 0.26 nm. Indeed, the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1 c) displayed the CD material underwent significant functionalization, resulting in the presence of a diverse range of chemical groups. The peaks observed at 3200 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1 were related to the telescopic vibrational of O-H and N-H bonds in CD. Additionally, the peak at 1690 cm−1 was attributed to the telescopic vibrational peak of the C-N bond. The spectrum of CD exhibited characteristic peaks of 1323 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 due to the telescopic vibrational peaks of B-O and CN groups. The IR band seen at 1020 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibrational peak of B-O-H groups. The 867 cm−1 and 663 cm−1 peaks were attributed to the asymmetric telescopic vibrational peak of C-O-C and the bending vibrational peak of O-C-O, respectively. The presence of B-OH, C-N and CN indicated N and B had been successfully introduced into CD as passivating agents.

The position of the emission peaks did not exhibit a substantial shift blue or red when the excitation wavelength was increased from 325 to 365 nm, as evidenced by the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1 d). However, the fluorescence intensity appeared to increase and then decrease. The maximum fluorescence intensity of CD occurred at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm when the emission peak of CD was 435 nm. As seen in Fig. 1 e, there were two obvious absorption peaks in the UV spectrum of CD. The absorption peak at 234 nm belonged to the CC π-π* transition and the absorption peak at 334 nm belonged to the CO n-π* transition. Fig. 1 f showed digital photographs of water and CD solutions taken under a 365 nm UV lamp. Under the UV light, the CD emitted a bright blue light.

The crystalline nature of CD was further characterized by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CD exhibited a dominant peak at 2θ=22.5, which originated from corresponding to graphitized amorphous carbon. The peaks were broad due to the small size of CDs and the high value of the interlayer distance, probably ascribed to the presence of oxygen functional groups. XPS was employed to determine further the functional groups in the material, as described in Fig. 2 b–f. The full spectrum showed typical peaks of the CD containing the N, O, B, and C elements. High-resolution peaks of O1s, N1s, C1s, and B1s were fitted separately. The N1s spectra indicated chemical bonds at 399, 400.9 eV and 402.8 eV, corresponding to C-N, N-H, and NO[45], respectively. The peaks at 523.3 eV and 530.1 eV in the O 1 s spectra indicated the presence of CO and C-O groups. The two B 1 s signals, associated with B-O and C-B, were also observed. In addition, the C 1 s spectra displayed three different peaks at 288.1, 284.6, and 287.1 eV, indicative of CO, C-C, and C-B [46], respectively. These outcomes were in agreement with FTIR results.

The zeta potentials of CD and ADP were found to be −18.6 and +12.5 mV, respectively, as tested by the nanoparticle-size potentiostat. Therefore, when ADP and CD were dispersed in acetone solution simultaneously, they could be attracted to each other and form ADP@CD under the condition of electrostatic interactions. The morphology of ADP@CD that CD was possibly adsorbed on the surface of the thin ADP sheets can be observed by SEM (Fig. S1), which further indicated that CD was successfully constructed on the ADP surface.

The cross-section morphology was observed after quenching the epoxy-cured material in liquid nitrogen. The distribution of ADP@CD in EP was investigated using SEM-EDS, as depicted in Fig. 3 a–c. The cross-section morphology of the epoxy resin underwent a gradual transition from smooth to rough following modification, indicating a shift in fracture mode from brittle fracture to ductile fracture. Furthermore, there was no discernible interfacial separation between ADP@CD and EP. Fig. S2 showed that the P, Al, and B elements were uniformly distributed on the EP surface, which indicated that ADP@CD was uniformly distributed in the EP thermosets. It further indicated that ADP@CD had good compatibility in EP.

As shown in Fig. 3 d, The variation of Tanδ with temperature for EP thermosets under programmed warming conditions. As a nanoparticle, CD had a certain restraining effect on the chain movement of the polymer molecular, which could shift the glass transition to higher temperatures and give the material a stronger elastic response, resulting in a decrease in Tanδ. With the addition of ADP@CD, the degree of EP cross-linking and the Tg increased significantly, and the Tanδ of the EP/ADP@CD thermosets was raised to 172 °C. The Tg of the EP/1ADP@0.5CD increased by 15.1 % compared to pure EP. The elevated Tg of EP thermosets resulting from the incorporation of ADP@CD primarily stems from its capacity to impede the mobility of the molecular chains.

Fig. 3 e–f presented the mechanical properties of EP and its thermosets, aiming to examine the effects of introducing ADP@CD on the tensile and impact properties. The tensile and impact strengths of all EP/ADP@CD thermosets remained essentially unchanged compared to EP, while the mechanical properties of EP/ADP@CD were superior to those of EP/ADP. For nanomaterial-filled polymers, under uniformly dispersed conditions, the nanoparticles led to enhanced interfacial interaction due to their large specific surface area or chemical bonding. As shown in Fig. S3, there was a clear aggregation of ADP in EP, whereas ADP@CD was more uniformly dispersed in EP. Therefore, the slightly improved mechanical properties of EP/ADP@CD may be due to the attractive interactions between CD and EP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity and electrical insulation analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal conductivity of EP thermosets was evaluated using the transient planar heat source (TPS) technique. The relationship between ADP@CD loading and thermal conductivity was visualized by the thermal conductivity, as shown in Fig. 4 a. Meanwhile, Fig. 4b assessed the rate at which the thermal conductivity of various EP materials increases in comparison to pure EP. The amorphous structure of Pure EP resulted in a low thermal conductivity of 0.298 Wm−1K−1, and the addition of ADP had no effect on the thermal conductivity of EP. This can be attributed to the discrete nature of each filler particle in EP, which prevented the establishment of an efficient heat channel. The thermal conductivity exhibited a slight increase when the quantity of filler injected was rather modest. In this study, a variety of particles with different particle sizes were mixed and added to the substrate. The packing density of thermosets could be effectively increased through the hybridization of fillers with varying sizes. Additionally, small fillers could penetrate the pores of larger fillers to create a continuous pathway for thermal conductivity and an ordered network structure in the substrate (Fig. S4). This increased the free range of phonon propagation and boosted the thermal conductivity of the thermosets. EP/1ADP@0.8CD had a thermal conductivity of 1.1 W·m−1·K−1, which was 302 % higher than pure EP. The high thermal conductivity allowed the heat of combustion to be transferred more quickly during combustion, which lowered the temperature around the burning material. Meanwhile, phonon scattering improved and the TC value dropped when the CD concentration increased further [47]. This could be because agglomeration inside the EP happened when too much CD was added, disrupting the heat transfer pathway.

To further investigate the thermal behavior of EP substances, a thermal infrared imager was used to record the changes in the surface temperature during the cooling and heating process and displayed in Fig. 4 c–f. The graph presented in Fig. 4 d showed that the temperature of pure EP increased more slowly on the heating table at 100°C and only rose to 65.8°C within 150 s. In contrast, EP/1ADP@0.8CD only required 150 s to warm up to 83.7 °C, and the surface temperature of the EP/1ADP@0.8CD thermosets changed noticeably more quickly than that of EP/1ADP. To validate the heat dissipation of EP composites, the cooling process of the samples was also analyzed. The surface temperature of pure EP decreased from 77 °C to 48.2°C within 150 s. The EP composite temperature decreased from 80.4 °C to 37.1 °C, and the surface color transitioned from red to blue. The low thermal conductivity of epoxy resins was primarily attributed to their limited ability to conduct phonons, along with the presence of external impurities and internal flaws that arise during the preparation and preservation process. These impurities and defects significantly disrupted the transit of phonons, hence impeding the efficient diffusion of heat. In contrast, the thermal conductivity of EP/ADP@CD thermosets with the introduction of CD doped with N atoms was enhanced. The introduction of N atoms [44] on the point defect side of CD can weaken the random thermal radiation of carbon atoms and create a high-frequency phonon tunnelling effect, which maintained the structural integrity of CD and prevented phonon scattering at the structural interface.

The impedance of the thermosets to current per unit volume increased the volume resistivity of the thermosets, which improved the insulating properties[48] of the thermosets. Generally speaking, volume resistivity in the range of 1010∼1022 could be called electrical insulation materials. Table 1 presented a summary of the findings regarding EP thermosets. The inclusion of fillers ADP and ADP@CD resulted in a volume resistivity over 1016Ω·m for all samples, which were in the insulating range. All EP/ADP@CD composites exhibited superior values compared to pure EP. The phenomenon meant that EP thermosets gained flame retardancy and high thermal conductivity while maintaining good electrical insulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of thermal stability and flame retardancy
ce:section.ce:para: The vertical combustion test results of pure EP and thermosets were shown in Fig. 5 a. The values of the associated LOI were also displayed in Fig. 5 b. There were no instances of molten droplets forming in any of the epoxy thermosets during combustion. Without flame retardant treatment, the LOI value of epoxy resin was only 26.5 %, which was extremely flammable. Once ignited, the flame was unable to self-extinguish until it had fully consumed itself and the combustion grade was NR. Although the EP/1ADP had an oxygen index of 35 %, the extinguishing time was around 50 s, which only achieved a V-1 rating. With the further addition of ADP@CD, the LOI of the thermosets continued to increase. EP/1ADP@0.5CD was able to self-extinguish within 5 s after ignition and achieved the LOI of 36.1 %. Additionally, the flammable combustion times t1 and t2 were dramatically lowered after two ignitions. The EP thermosets still achieved the V-0 level at relatively low flame retardant additions, suggesting the demonstration of excellent flame retardancy of ADP@CD.

The TG and DTG data of pure EP, CD and EP thermosets under the N2 atmosphere were shown in Fig.S5 and Table 2. The TGA curve of CD exhibited an initial inflection point at a temperature of 103 °C. The primary cause for the onset of the significant reduction in mass at this juncture may be attributed to the breakdown of the absorbed water [40]. The second inflection point in the TGA curve of CD was observed around 279 °C, potentially attributed to the breakdown of a portion of the amorphous carbon and the segregation of impurities. At a temperature of 800 °C, the CDs had a residual mass of 44.57 %, indicating a significantly high char residue. Pure EP started to decompose at 361.37 °C and the residual char rate was only 14.44 % at 800 °C. The addition of ADP decreased the initial decomposition temperature of materials, following the catalytic effect of the Lewis acid-base principle on matrix polymers as reported in the study by Ulrike et al. [51]. As metal cations, aluminum ions played the role of Lewis acids, which could take away the molecules containing electron-pairing groups in polymers and accelerate the decomposition of polymers under certain temperature conditions with certain catalytic effects. On the other hand, the addition of ADP@CD resulted in a residual char rate of 19.96 % for the EP substrate. This was due to the low thermal stability of CD, which inhibited the transfer of mass and heat by degrading and catalyzing the formation of a char layer in the low-temperature zone. The addition of ADP@CD together promoted the thermal degradation of EP at elevated temperatures[42] and contributed to the formation of residual carbon[43]. Compared to neat EP, the maximum mass loss rate of EP thermosets was greatly reduced, which effectively prevented further burning of the EP substrate.

In order to further investigate the flame retardant properties of EP thermosets, cone volume tests were carried out. The HRR, THR, COP, and TSP curves of EP and flame retardant EP thermosets were shown in Fig. 6 a–f and Table S2. The TTI of EP, EP/ADP and EP/1ADP@0.5CD were 54 s, 32 s, and 51 s, respectively, indicating that ADP@CD was preferentially decomposed in the thermosets system, which in turn improved the flame retardant properties of the materials. Fig. 6 a depicted the heat release curve of EP thermosets, which exhibited two distinct peaks. The appearance occurred due to the ignition of EP thermosets, which resulted in the liberation of gases from the thermal decomposition of ADP@CD. These gases formed a protective layer of char layer, effectively inhibiting the release of heat. However, the initial formation of the char layer was fragile. With the increasing temperature, the carbon layer broke and the second peak appeared in the curve. Without adding any flame retardant, the pHRR of neat EP was as high as 1335 kW/m2, while adding ADP@CD reduced the pHRR to 1091 kW/m2. The addition of ADP@CD allowed EP to rapidly promote the generation of a secondary char layer after the primary char layer had broken down, which in turn prevented heat dissipation and smoke release.

As can be seen from Fig. 6 d, the TSP of pure EP was 39 kW/m2, while the reduction of EP/ADP@CD reached 25.6 %. The above results showed that adding ADP@CD had a certain flue gas inhibition effect on the combustion of EP. The cause of this phenomenon may be attributed to the nano-barrier effect of CD, which hindered the transfer of heat between the substrate and the flame region. As a result, the likelihood of smoke particles colliding with char residue was increased, leading to a decrease in the release rate of pyrolysis volatiles. This effectively slowed down the escape of volatile pyrolysis products. Smoke density test (Fig. S6) was to measure the change of light transmission intensity before and after the incident smoke by using the extinction principle to characterize the smoke concentration. The specific optical density (Ds) of EP thermosets decreased with the addition of ADP@CD, which implied a continuous decrease in smoke density, proving that appropriate levels of ADP@CD increase the chances of people escaping from a fire. A comparison of the flame retardant properties of the current study and EP thermosets reported in recent years was shown in Fig. 5 c. The study was at the forefront of its field in terms of low CO2 emissions and exceptionally low loadings of flame retardants on the basis of meeting UL 94 V-0. Thus, ADP@CD had excellent effect on the flame retardation of EP.

A large number of fire accidents were caused by people inhaling smoke and toxic gases during escape. Consequently, measuring the emissions of CO and CO2 was the critical factor for assessing fire risk. The incomplete combustion of flame-retardant EP by the addition of ADP resulting in an increase in CO production and a decrease in CO2 production. As shown in Table S2 and Fig. 6 e–f, compared with EP/1ADP, the COP of EP/1.5ADP@CD cured products decreased by 21.4 % and CO2P decreased by 14.7 %, indicating that ADP@CD was significantly helpful to suppress the emission of epoxy resin smoke and toxic gases.
(1) FPI = TTI / pHRR
(2) FGI = pHRR / Time to reach pHRR

The fire performance index (FPI) and fire growth index (FGI) were calculated from Eqs. (1) and (2), further highlighting the fire safety of EP thermosets. EP/ADP@CD exhibited the lowest flame propagation during combustion and the highest flame propagation index, indicating a reduced fire danger.

The butane torch was positioned 7 cm away from the sample and the tip of the flame was placed against the direct center of the sample. The flame was continuously applied until the epoxy substrate was burnt through, and the temperature and heat distribution on the backside of the sample was recorded by an infrared thermographic camera, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . The time to ignite (TTI) of the epoxy thermosets was extended in comparison to pure epoxy. The backside temperature at the instant of ignition was relatively high for EP/ADP@CD compared to EP/ADP. As combustion proceeded, the unfilled resin began to crack at 55 s and the EP/ADP lost its structural integrity at 82 s. In the pre-combustion phase, EP/ADP@CD had a higher backside temperature and a larger thermal coverage area compared to EP/ADP for the same combustion time. This is due to the fact that EP/ADP@CD had good thermal conductivity and was able to dissipate heat quickly in all directions. Furthermore, when both EP and EP/ADP@CD reached the burning temperature threshold (BTT), the thermal barrier (TB) of the EP/ADP@CD was comparatively lower. This was because the inclusion of ADP@CD encouraged the breakdown of EP, and the presence of N and B elements in ADP@CD resulted in the formation of a dense and stable char residue during EP combustion. This char residue effectively hindered the transfer and propagation of high flame temperatures to the backside of EP.

Fig. 8 a–f displayed the overhead photograph and microscopic structure in the residual char of EP substances following combustion. The neat EP almost disappeared after burning, and the residual char surface had a large number of holes. In contrast, both EP/ADP and EP/ADP@CD formed more intact expanded residual char after combustion. The distribution of elemental composition in the remaining char after the combustion of EP and its composites can be shown in Fig. 8 j and Fig. S7. ADP can undergo dehydration to generate phosphates, and these phosphate particles were uniformly distributed throughout the char layer, promoting the enhancement of both the antioxidant capacity and barrier function of the char layer. However, the relatively low ADP addition resulted in these residual char being more fragile. As the temperature increased, the weak residual char formed by ADP gradually broke down and formed pores again, so the heat release from EP/ADP appeared to decrease and then increase. The P and Al elements were more evenly distributed on the surface of char layer and the thickness and densification of the char layer were significantly enhanced by the addition of ADP@CD. The presence of highly graphitized CD reduced the heat transfer from the surface and promoted the production of large amounts of carbon, which acted as an insulator and oxygen barrier. The complete char layer protected the substrate underneath by effectively inhibiting the escape of heat, the movement of flammable gasses, and the emission of smoke particles The oxides containing boron and phosphorus produced by the combustion decomposition of ADP@CD had good thermal-oxidative stability and prevented further oxidation of the substrate, which led to a high residual char rate.

The degree of graphitization of the residual char was further investigated by Raman spectroscopy, as indicated in Fig. 8 g–i. The degree of defects and disorder in the carbon material was demonstrated by the ID peak at 1360 cm−1, while the degree of order and lattice vibrations were indicated by the IG peak at 1600 cm−1, which represented the in-plane stretching vibrations of the SP2 hybridization of the carbon atoms. The ID/IG of pure EP was 3.12, whereas the ratios of EP to ADP and EP to ADP at CD were decreased to 2.66 and 2.26, respectively. The results showed that the char layer produced by burning EP/ADP@CD had a greater level of graphitization compared to the remaining char from pure EP. Additionally, this char layer acted as a more effective insulator against the surrounding air.

Py-GC/MS investigated the role of ADP@CD in the thermal degradation of EP [52]. Based on the NIST library and related literature [53,54], Table 3 and Fig. 9 summarized typical fragments and contents of EP/ADP@CD thermal cleavage products. It can be found that 20 kinds of relatively strong chromatographic peaks were generated during the thermal cleavage of EP/1ADP@CD with retention times ranging from 1.66 min to 25.32 min. Meanwhile, the majority of products had benzene rings, which could potentially impede the combustion of the substrate in the gaseous state. Overall, as the length of retained time increased, there was a gradual rise in the molecular weight of the discovered compounds. During the initial thermal cleavage stage, EP was first degraded to form structures such as phenol (peak 4), p-Cumenol (peak 7) and 4-[1-(4-methoxyphenyl)-1-methylethyl]phenol (peak 10). At 12.41 min, the EP thermosets showed a peak corresponding to phosphophenol, which was caused by the flame retardant affected by ADP in the epoxy resin. The partial decomposition of CD produced N-containing atoms or groups and then generated nonflammable gases such as N2 and NH3, which can be used to dilute the concentration of combustible gases generated during the combustion of the substrate, thus inhibiting the combustion.

A comparison of the gaseous product production by EP and EP/ADP@CD was presented in Fig. 10 and Fig. S8. It was observed that the inclusion of ADP@CD resulted in a substantial decrease in the quantity of gaseous products in thermosets. EP and EP/ADP@CD basically did not undergo thermal decomposition before 300 ℃, and the thermal decomposition process gradually ended after 450 ℃. EP and EP/1ADP@0.5CD decomposed quickly at 380°C, but the products of thermal broke down progressively diminished between 450°C and 650°C. With the continuous increase of temperature, neat EP continued to decompose into trace amounts of ketones [55], aldehydes and ethers. Furthermore, the breakdown of EP thermosets had essentially stopped at roughly 650 °C. This is due to the possibility of ADP@CD being thermally cracked in the epoxy resin, which created a protective char layer to cover the EP surface and prevented EP from further degrading in the high-temperature area. On the other hand, it can be seen from the figure that the addition of ADP@CD successfully suppressed the emission of flammable gases such as hydrocarbons (2930 cm−1), CO (2172 cm−1), CO2 (2308 cm−1), and NH3 (930 cm−1)[56], in comparison to pure EP. Reducing the quantity of flammable gases emitted decreases the likelihood of a fire occurring. Simultaneously, the significant decrease in aromatic compounds (1508 cm−1), which served as precursors to smoke particles, contributed to the reduction in smoke production during the combustion process. The ether compounds (1174 cm−1) showed the same decreasing trend, which was beneficial in reducing the toxicity of the material smoke.

The potential flame retardant mechanism of EP/ADP@CD was illustrated in Scheme 2. Due to the well dispersion of ADP@CD in EP and the presence of N/B groups on the surface of CD, the addition of ADP@CD can effectively catalyze carbonation and accelerate the early disintegration of EP during combustion. The hypophosphite produced during the pyrolysis of ADP can function as a mild Lewis base and the electron-deficient boron atom contained in CD could act as a Lewis acid towards the electron-containing pyrolysis molecule with free radicals. The formation of acid-base complexes could promote the breaking of epoxy bonds to terminate the pyrolysis reaction. The P· and PO· radicals produced by ADP decomposition in the gas phase could capture the H· and HO· produced by combustion, thus preventing the free radical chain reaction produced by EP combustion. The concentration of combustible gases in the gas phase could be decreased by non-combustible gases such as NH3 and N2, which were produced by the decomposition of CD during the early stages of combustion. Simultaneously, ADP@CD made the thermal conductivity of EP thermosets much higher, and the heat dissipated rapidly in all directions during the combustion process, significantly minimizing the accumulation of heat.

In summary, N, B-doped CD was successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal process and constructed ADP on the surface of CD. The multifunctional EP was successfully fabricated by incorporating ADP@CD as flame retardant and thermally conductive filler. Due to the great dispersion performance between ADP@CD and the EP matrix, EP/ADP@CD exhibited high thermal conductivity. Remarkably, the incorporation of ADP@CD made EP/ADP@CD to be highly flame-retardant, which easily passed the UL-94 V-0 rating and achieved 36.2 % LOI values higher than neat EP at a low ADP@CD content. Due to the catalytic char formation and free radical scavenging ability of ADP@CD, the total smoke release and heat release rate of EP/ADP@CD thermosets were significantly reduced in the cone test. The TG test revealed that the residual char of EP/1ADP@0.5CD was higher than that of the pure epoxy resin. The study revealed that the flame retardant effect of ADP@CD was primarily a result of its combined action in both the gas phase and condensed phase flame retardancy. This study enhances flame retardancy, reduces smoke toxicity, improves thermal conductivity, and enhances electrical insulation properties of EP at a relatively modest addition level of 1.5 wt%, offering a straightforward and environmentally benign method for producing multifunctional epoxy resins.

Yang Leng:Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Peixiao Sun:Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Hongjian Zhang:Data curation. Zhao Wang:Writing – review & editing. Jun Zhang:Writing – review & editing. Miaojun Xu:Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Xiaoli Li:Writing – review & editing, Validation. Bin Li:Supervision, Resources.
